text.heading: President under pressure to terminate $8.3b contract with Chinese firm
text.p: From Yusuf Alli, Managing Editor (Northern Operation)
President Umaru Yar’Adua is under pressure to terminate an $8.3billion rail modernisation contract sealed by his predecessor’s administration with a Chinese company, The Nation has learnt.
The President is caught between advisers in government prodding him to pull the brakes on the deal and those urging restraint.
But Chinese President Hu Jingtao has weighed in, appealing to Yar’Adua to retain the contract.

Former President Olusegun Obasanjo’s administration, in October 2006, awarded the railway modernisation project to the China Civil Engineering Construction Corporation (CCECC). The consultancy component of the contract, worth about $240million, was awarded to Technics Engineering Architecting Marketing Nigeria Limited (TEAM).

A document obtained from the Budget Monitoring and Price Intelligence Unit (BMPIU) of the Presidency shows that the project involves the survey, design, construction and maintenance of 1,315 kilometres of double-track standard gauge rail line from Lagos to Kano.
Although the CCECC initially presented a bill of $15.411billion for the project, the contract cost was revised to $8.3billion by the BMPIU.

But some top officials of the Yar’Adua administration have expressed concern over the project cost and the manner in which the contract was awarded by the former administration. While some alleged that the contract was inflated to $8.3billion from $5 .billion, some others claimed that due process was sidelined.
There is however another view among technocrats, including the TEAM consultants, that the cost is comparable to such a project in any part of the world.

The Nation learnt yesterday that the project has caused a division in the Yar’Adua’s government as some ministers want the contract revoked; whereas others – notably top officials of the Federal Ministry of Transportation and the Nigerian Railway Corporation management – have urged restraint. Finance Minister Shamsudeen Usman and his Transportation counterpart, Mrs. Diezani Allison-Madueke, are in the vanguard of those pushing for termination of the contract.

The controversy had prompted President Yar’Adua to raise an 11-man Presidential Committee on Funding of the Railway Modernisation Project. Our correspondent learnt that the panel, headed by the Minister of Finance, had recommended many options to the President – including contract termination.

Other options recommended by the committee, according to a Presidency document, included: reducing the scope of the contract to single track, prolonging the contract duration from four to seven years, phased implementation of the project to reduce the financial burden on government, and sticking to the initial terms of the contract on funding.

A source at the Presidency said: "Since the committee submitted its report in January, there has been intense pressure on the President to terminate the contract. Left to Yar’Adua, he wants to continue with the project; when the presidential committee on funding submitted its report, he took exception to the recommendation that the contract should be terminated. But there has been intense lobbying by some powerful groups for contract termination.

"In the midst of the lobbying, the Ministry of Justice presented a brief to the President that there was no clause for termination of the contract in the agreement signed by a Presidential Negotiation Committee headed by then Private Secretary to ex-President Obasanjo, Mr. Stephen Orosanye. But the Minister of Transportation has countered that Clause 15.5 in the contract made provision for termination."
The lobby groups are said to be proposing a $2billion rehabilitation scheme for the railway, which would largely be awarded as contract patronage of party loyalists.
Presidency sources disclosed that Chinese President Jingtao has written a letter to his Nigerian counterpart, appealing that the contract be retained.

One source said: "The contract is assuming a diplomatic dimension. This is the first time that the Chinese President would interfere in a business matter in Nigeria. You can imagine the pressure on the President. His predecessor, Obasanjo, is also unhappy that a project he conceived is about to be terminated."
Contacted on what informed the letter from the Chinese president, the Chief Coordinator of CCECC, Shi Hongbing, told our correspondent: "I cannot talk to you on the issue; I am out of the country."
Railway workers yesterday in Lagos vowed to resist further cut-back on the corporation’s workforce, which has already been trimmed to 6,514 from 42,000.

The president of the NRC chapter of the Senior Staff Association of Communications, Transport and Corporations (SSACTAC), Mr. Abiodun Lesi, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) that repositioning the company required structural reforms; not further staff downsizing.
Lesi was reacting to allegations that the Federal Government has concluded plans to sack about 1,000 workers of the corporation. He said any further staff retrenchment would be unacceptable.
